facts.quizQuestion: The Grand Canyon stretches along the Colorado River — how long is it?
facts.explanation: The Grand Canyon in Arizona, USA, is 277 miles (446 km) long, up to 18 miles wide, and reaches a maximum depth of 1,857 meters. The Colorado River carved it over 5–6 million years, exposing nearly 2 billion years of Earth's geological history.
facts.statement: The Grand Canyon is 277 miles (446 km) long and up to 1,857 meters deep.
facts.wowFactor: The canyon walls expose rock layers spanning 2 billion years — nearly half the age of Earth. The bottom layer, the Vishnu Basement Rocks, formed long before complex life existed.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art aerial view of a vast red-and-orange canyon with a winding blue river at the bottom under a bright desert sky

facts.quizQuestion: Which Spanish explorer became the first European to see the Grand Canyon in 1540?
facts.explanation: García López de Cárdenas, a lieutenant in Francisco Vásquez de Coronado's expedition, reached the rim of the Grand Canyon in 1540. He was guided there by Hopi people and spent three days trying unsuccessfully to descend to the Colorado River below.
facts.statement: Spanish explorer García López de Cárdenas became the first European to see the Grand Canyon in 1540.
facts.wowFactor: After Cárdenas's visit, Europeans largely ignored the canyon for over 200 years. The next recorded European visit was in 1776 — more than two centuries later.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Spanish conquistador in armor standing at a canyon rim, gazing into a vast red gorge

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did the Grand Canyon become a US National Park?
facts.explanation: President Woodrow Wilson signed the Grand Canyon National Park Act on February 26, 1919, establishing it as the 17th US National Park. Theodore Roosevelt had declared it a National Monument in 1908, protecting it years before it received full National Park status.
facts.statement: The Grand Canyon became a US National Park in 1919.
facts.wowFactor: Teddy Roosevelt visited the Grand Canyon in 1903 and famously said: 'Leave it as it is. You cannot improve on it. The ages have been at work on it, and man can only mar it.'
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a vintage National Park sign in front of a sweeping canyon panorama under a golden sunset

facts.quizQuestion: Stretching along the Queensland coast, approximately how long is the Great Barrier Reef?
facts.explanation: The Great Barrier Reef stretches approximately 2,300 km along the northeastern coast of Queensland, Australia. It comprises over 2,900 individual reefs and 900 islands, covering an area of 344,400 km². It became a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1981.
facts.statement: The Great Barrier Reef stretches 2,300 km along the Queensland coast of Australia.
facts.wowFactor: The Great Barrier Reef is the largest structure on Earth built by living organisms — the coral polyps that form it are tiny animals, and the reef they have collectively built is so large it is visible from space.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a vibrant coral reef underwater with hundreds of colorful fish, viewed from above showing Queensland's coastline

facts.quizQuestion: The Great Barrier Reef is not one reef but a system of how many individual reefs?
facts.explanation: The Great Barrier Reef is composed of over 2,900 individual reefs plus more than 900 islands. It covers an area of 344,400 km², making it the world's largest coral reef system and the largest structure made by living organisms on Earth.
facts.statement: The Great Barrier Reef is composed of over 2,900 individual reefs and 900 islands.
facts.wowFactor: Despite being the world's largest living structure, coral polyps — the animals that build the reef — are typically only a few millimeters wide. The Great Barrier Reef is billions of them working together over thousands of years.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art top-down map view of the Queensland coast showing thousands of tiny reef patches in shades of turquoise and blue

facts.quizQuestion: What is the local Kololo-language name for Victoria Falls, meaning 'The Smoke That Thunders'?
facts.explanation: Victoria Falls is known as 'Mosi-oa-Tunya' in the Kololo language, meaning 'The Smoke That Thunders.' The spray from the falls — which is 1,708 meters wide and 108 meters tall — can be seen from up to 50 km away, looking like rising smoke.
facts.statement: Victoria Falls is called 'Mosi-oa-Tunya' — 'The Smoke That Thunders' — in the local Kololo language.
facts.wowFactor: The spray from Victoria Falls creates a permanent rain forest in the surrounding area and can be seen 50 km away. During peak flow, over 500 million liters of water per minute plunge over the edge.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a massive waterfall sending a column of white mist high into the sky, visible across a flat African savanna

facts.quizQuestion: Which explorer named Victoria Falls in 1855, after the British queen?
facts.explanation: Scottish explorer David Livingstone became the first European to see Victoria Falls on November 16, 1855, and named it after Queen Victoria. He described it as 'scenes so lovely must have been gazed upon by angels in their flight.' The falls sit on the Zambia-Zimbabwe border.
facts.statement: David Livingstone named Victoria Falls in 1855 after Queen Victoria.
facts.wowFactor: Livingstone approached the falls by canoe with local guides, nearly going over the edge. He carved his initials on a small island now called Livingstone Island, right at the lip of the main falls.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a Victorian-era explorer in a pith helmet standing at a canyon edge overlooking a thundering waterfall

facts.quizQuestion: Victoria Falls on the Zambia-Zimbabwe border is the world's largest curtain of falling water — how wide is it?
facts.explanation: Victoria Falls is 1,708 meters wide and 108 meters tall. Its average flow is 1,088 cubic meters per second. This combination of width and height makes it the world's largest sheet of falling water — larger than Niagara Falls by volume.
facts.statement: Victoria Falls is 1,708 meters wide, making it the world's widest curtain of falling water.
facts.wowFactor: During the flood season, Victoria Falls has the highest flow rate of any waterfall on Earth. The roar can be heard 40 km away, and the spray soaks visitors standing on the opposite rim of the gorge.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art wide-angle view of a mile-wide waterfall plunging into a gorge, with a rainbow forming in the permanent mist cloud

facts.quizQuestion: Per the 2020 joint China-Nepal survey, how tall is Mount Everest?
facts.explanation: The 2020 joint China-Nepal survey established Mount Everest's official height at 8,848.86 meters (29,031.7 ft) above sea level. This replaced the previous figure of 8,848 m set in 1954. Everest sits on the China-Nepal border and grows approximately 4 mm per year from tectonic uplift.
facts.statement: Mount Everest is 8,848.86 meters tall, per the 2020 survey.
facts.wowFactor: Everest grows about 4 mm per year due to tectonic uplift as the Indian Plate pushes under the Eurasian Plate — the same collision that created the entire Himalayas.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a snow-capped pyramid peak towering above surrounding mountains, with the Tibetan plateau stretching behind it

facts.quizQuestion: When did Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay first reach the summit of Mount Everest?
facts.explanation: On May 29, 1953, New Zealander Edmund Hillary and Nepali-Indian Sherpa Tenzing Norgay became the first people confirmed to have reached the summit of Mount Everest. They were part of a British expedition led by John Hunt. The news reached London just in time for Queen Elizabeth II's coronation.
facts.statement: Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay first summited Everest on May 29, 1953.
facts.wowFactor: News of the first summit was transmitted by coded message and reached London on the morning of Queen Elizabeth II's coronation — June 2, 1953. The papers announced it as a coronation gift to the new queen.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of two climbers in heavy winter gear planting a flag on a snowy peak at sunrise, surrounded by clouds far below

facts.quizQuestion: What is the Nepali name for Mount Everest, meaning 'Goddess of the Sky'?
facts.explanation: Mount Everest is called Sagarmatha in Nepali (meaning 'Goddess of the Sky' or 'Head of the Earth-Ocean') and Qomolangma in Tibetan (meaning 'Goddess Mother of the World'). The name 'Everest' was given by the Royal Geographical Society in 1865 after Surveyor General Sir George Everest.
facts.statement: Mount Everest's Nepali name is Sagarmatha; its Tibetan name is Qomolangma.
facts.wowFactor: Sir George Everest himself opposed naming the mountain after him — he argued it couldn't be written in Hindi or spoken by local people. He was overruled, and the name was adopted anyway.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a decorated Himalayan temple in the foreground with the snow-capped peak of Everest towering in the background

facts.quizQuestion: At what altitude in the atmosphere do the Northern Lights (aurora borealis) occur?
facts.explanation: The Northern Lights (aurora borealis) occur at altitudes of 100–300 km above Earth's surface, when charged solar wind particles collide with oxygen and nitrogen atoms in the upper atmosphere. Different gases produce different colors: oxygen at ~100 km produces green, oxygen at higher altitude produces red, and nitrogen produces blue-purple.
facts.statement: The Northern Lights occur 100–300 km above Earth's surface, where solar particles excite atmospheric gases.
facts.wowFactor: The different colors of the Northern Lights come from different gases at different heights: green (most common) is oxygen at ~100 km; red is oxygen above 200 km; blue and purple are nitrogen. The rarest aurora color is all-red.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art night sky over a snowy arctic landscape with green and purple ribbons of light undulating across the stars

facts.quizQuestion: What natural phenomenon causes the Northern Lights when it interacts with Earth's atmosphere?
facts.explanation: The Northern Lights (aurora borealis) are caused by solar wind — streams of charged particles (mainly electrons and protons) from the Sun. When these particles are funneled toward the poles by Earth's magnetic field and collide with oxygen and nitrogen at 100–300 km altitude, they excite those atoms, which release energy as visible light.
facts.statement: The Northern Lights are caused by solar wind particles exciting oxygen and nitrogen in Earth's upper atmosphere.
facts.wowFactor: The Southern Lights (aurora australis) occur simultaneously at the opposite magnetic pole. Astronauts on the ISS can observe both auroras from above — seeing the glowing rings around both poles at once.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art scientific diagram showing the Sun blasting a stream of particles toward Earth, with glowing curtains appearing near both poles

facts.quizQuestion: Giant's Causeway in Northern Ireland is famous for its interlocking basalt columns — approximately how many are there?
facts.explanation: Giant's Causeway in County Antrim, Northern Ireland, consists of approximately 40,000 interlocking basalt columns, formed by a Paleogene volcanic eruption 50–60 million years ago. Most columns are hexagonal, the tallest reaching 12 meters. It became a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1986.
facts.statement: Giant's Causeway consists of approximately 40,000 interlocking basalt columns, mostly hexagonal.
facts.wowFactor: According to Irish legend, the giant Fionn mac Cumhaill built the causeway as a bridge to Scotland to fight his Scottish rival Benandonner. The Scottish side is the island of Staffa, which has similar hexagonal columns — the same ancient lava flow.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of thousands of perfectly hexagonal black basalt columns stepping out into a grey-green sea under overcast skies

facts.quizQuestion: Which legendary Irish giant is said to have built the Giant's Causeway to walk to Scotland?
facts.explanation: Irish legend says the giant Fionn mac Cumhaill (Finn McCool) built the Giant's Causeway as a bridge to Scotland so he could fight his rival, the Scottish giant Benandonner. The scientific explanation is far older: a Paleogene volcanic eruption 50–60 million years ago caused rapid cooling that cracked the lava into hexagonal columns.
facts.statement: Irish legend credits giant Fionn mac Cumhaill with building the Giant's Causeway to walk to Scotland.
facts.wowFactor: The island of Staffa off Scotland's west coast has near-identical hexagonal basalt columns — both were part of the same ancient lava flow. Fingal's Cave on Staffa was part of the same eruption, inspiring Mendelssohn's famous overture.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a massive giant striding across hexagonal stepping stones over the sea toward misty cliffs on the horizon

facts.quizQuestion: On what date did the Paricutín volcano begin erupting from a cornfield in Michoacán, Mexico?
facts.explanation: On February 20, 1943, a new volcano began erupting from a crack in farmer Dionisio Pulido's cornfield in Michoacán, Mexico. The eruption continued until 1952, and in nine years Paricutín grew to approximately 424 meters tall, burying the town of San Juan Parangaricutiro. It is one of the few volcanoes in history whose birth was witnessed.
facts.statement: Paricutín volcano erupted from a farmer's cornfield on February 20, 1943.
facts.wowFactor: Farmer Dionisio Pulido was plowing his cornfield when he felt the ground trembling. A crack opened, smoke began rising, and by the following morning a cone of ash and rock was already 10 meters high. Within a week it was taller than a five-story building.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a conical volcano rising from a flat cornfield, with lava and ash pouring out, a farmer fleeing in the foreground

facts.quizQuestion: Which Mexican farmer witnessed the birth of the Paricutín volcano on his property in 1943?
facts.explanation: Dionisio Pulido was plowing his cornfield in Michoacán, Mexico, on February 20, 1943, when a crack opened in the ground and smoke began pouring out. Within nine years, the volcano that erupted from his field grew to 424 meters tall and buried the nearby town of San Juan Parangaricutiro. Pulido received $1,500 in compensation and a small pension.
facts.statement: Farmer Dionisio Pulido witnessed the birth of Paricutín volcano from his cornfield in 1943.
facts.wowFactor: The church tower of San Juan Parangaricutiro still protrudes from the hardened lava that buried the town — a surreal landmark where people can walk across black rock to a lone tower sticking out of the ground.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a bewildered farmer in a straw hat watching a smoking fissure open in his brown cornfield under a clear blue sky

facts.quizQuestion: The Great Blue Hole off the coast of Belize is a giant underwater sinkhole — how deep is it?
facts.explanation: The Great Blue Hole is a submarine sinkhole located at Lighthouse Reef, Belize. It is 318 meters across and 124 meters deep, and was formed as a limestone cave system during ice ages when sea levels were much lower. At 40 meters depth, stalactites prove it was once a dry cave. Jacques Cousteau made it famous in 1971.
facts.statement: The Great Blue Hole off Belize is 318 meters wide and 124 meters deep.
facts.wowFactor: The stalactites found at 40 meters underwater inside the Great Blue Hole can only form in air — proving the cave was dry when sea levels were much lower during the ice ages. As the glaciers melted, the ocean flooded in.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art aerial view of a perfect circular deep blue hole in the middle of a shallow turquoise reef, surrounded by barrier reefs

facts.quizQuestion: Which famous oceanographer made the Great Blue Hole world-famous after diving there in 1971?
facts.explanation: French oceanographer Jacques Cousteau made the Great Blue Hole famous when he dived it in 1971 with his vessel Calypso, declaring it one of the top five dive sites in the world. The Great Blue Hole is part of the Belize Barrier Reef Reserve System, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
facts.statement: Jacques Cousteau dived the Great Blue Hole in 1971 and declared it one of the top five dive sites in the world.
facts.wowFactor: The Great Blue Hole looks dark blue from above because it is extremely deep — the shallow surrounding reef appears turquoise, but the sinkhole plunges 124 meters, making the water appear almost black in the middle from altitude.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a research vessel anchored above a perfectly circular deep-blue void in a bright turquoise reef, with scuba divers descending into the darkness

facts.quizQuestion: The Dead Sea is famous for extreme saltiness — what is its approximate salinity?
facts.explanation: The Dead Sea has a salinity of approximately 34.2%, making it about 9.6 times saltier than ordinary ocean water (which is ~3.5% salt). This extreme salinity makes the water so dense (1.24 kg/litre) that humans float effortlessly without swimming. The Dead Sea sits on the Jordan-Israel-West Bank border and is shrinking at about 1 meter per year.
facts.statement: The Dead Sea has ~34.2% salinity — about 9.6 times saltier than average ocean water.
facts.wowFactor: The Dead Sea is 439.78 meters below sea level — the lowest point on Earth's surface. It is also one of the world's saltiest bodies of water, though it trails behind Don Juan Pond in Antarctica at 44% salinity.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a person effortlessly reading a newspaper while floating upright in a brilliant blue lake surrounded by white salt crystals on the shore

facts.quizQuestion: How far below sea level is the Dead Sea, Earth's lowest land point?
facts.explanation: The Dead Sea sits 439.78 meters below sea level, making the shoreline the lowest point on Earth's land surface. It borders Jordan to the east and Israel and the West Bank to the west, and is shrinking by approximately 1 meter per year due to water diversion from the Jordan River.
facts.statement: The Dead Sea is 439.78 meters below sea level — Earth's lowest land point.
facts.wowFactor: The Dead Sea is disappearing. It loses about 1 meter of depth each year as water is diverted from the Jordan River for agriculture and drinking. Sinkholes are opening up along the former shoreline where freshwater is seeping in and dissolving underground salt layers.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art elevation cross-section diagram showing the Dead Sea surface way below the sea level reference line, with the Jordan River flowing in from the top

acceptableAlternatives: Rio de Janeiro means River of January
facts.quizQuestion: Portuguese explorers in 1502 named the Harbour of Rio de Janeiro — what does 'Rio de Janeiro' literally mean?
facts.explanation: When Portuguese explorers entered Guanabara Bay in January 1502, they mistook the wide bay for the mouth of a great river and named it 'Rio de Janeiro' — meaning 'River of January.' The city that grew around the bay inherited the name. Guanabara Bay is 412 km², 31 km long, and 28 km wide.
facts.statement: 'Rio de Janeiro' literally means 'River of January' — Portuguese explorers mistook Guanabara Bay for a river mouth.
facts.wowFactor: Sugarloaf Mountain (Pão de Açúcar) stands 396 meters tall at the entrance to Guanabara Bay. Its unusual shape was used by Portuguese sailors as a navigation landmark for centuries.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a 16th-century Portuguese caravel sailing into a vast blue bay surrounded by dramatic jungle-covered mountains

facts.quizQuestion: How tall is Sugarloaf Mountain (Pão de Açúcar), the iconic peak at the entrance to Guanabara Bay, Rio de Janeiro?
facts.explanation: Sugarloaf Mountain (Pão de Açúcar) rises 396 meters above Guanabara Bay in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil. It is a granite and quartz monolith and one of the seven Natural Wonders associated with the Harbour of Rio de Janeiro. A cable car has run to its summit since 1912.
facts.statement: Sugarloaf Mountain at the entrance to Guanabara Bay is 396 meters tall.
facts.wowFactor: The name 'Pão de Açúcar' (Sugarloaf) refers to the shape of refined sugar loaves used in colonial-era Brazil and Portugal. The peak's distinctive silhouette has appeared on Brazilian imagery since the colonial period.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a rounded granite peak rising sharply from the edge of a blue bay, with a cable car suspended against an orange sunset sky

facts.quizQuestion: The Great Barrier Reef is Earth's largest structure made by living organisms — what creatures built it?
facts.explanation: The Great Barrier Reef is built by coral polyps — tiny animals that secrete calcium carbonate skeletons. Over thousands of years, countless generations of polyps have accumulated to form the 2,300 km reef. Each polyp is only a few millimeters wide, but their collective structure is visible from space.
facts.statement: The Great Barrier Reef is built by coral polyps, tiny animals that secrete calcium carbonate skeletons.
facts.wowFactor: Coral is an animal, not a plant. Each tiny coral polyp feeds at night by extending tentacles to catch plankton. The algae (zooxanthellae) living inside coral give it color and provide up to 90% of its energy through photosynthesis — which is why coral bleaches white when it expels the algae under stress.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art close-up of colorful coral polyps with extended tentacles in a vivid underwater reef, surrounded by tropical fish

facts.quizQuestion: When did the volcanic eruption occur that created Giant's Causeway in Northern Ireland?
facts.explanation: Giant's Causeway was formed during a Paleogene volcanic eruption approximately 50–60 million years ago. When vast quantities of molten basalt lava rapidly cooled and contracted, it cracked into the characteristic hexagonal columns. About 40,000 of these columns remain today, the tallest reaching 12 meters.
facts.statement: Giant's Causeway was formed by volcanic eruptions 50–60 million years ago during the Paleogene period.
facts.wowFactor: The same volcanic eruption that created Giant's Causeway also formed Fingal's Cave on the Scottish island of Staffa — nearly identical hexagonal basalt columns that inspired Felix Mendelssohn's Hebrides Overture.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cross-section diagram showing lava cooling into perfectly geometric hexagonal columns below a prehistoric sky with volcanic activity

facts.quizQuestion: The Grand Canyon's rock layers expose how many years of Earth's geological history?
facts.explanation: The Grand Canyon's walls expose approximately 2 billion years of Earth's geological history. The oldest rocks at the canyon bottom — the Vishnu Basement Rocks — formed nearly 2 billion years ago. The Colorado River began carving the canyon 5–6 million years ago, gradually exposing these ancient layers.
facts.statement: The Grand Canyon exposes approximately 2 billion years of Earth's geological history in its rock layers.
facts.wowFactor: The youngest rocks at the top of the Grand Canyon's rim are about 270 million years old. The oldest at the bottom are nearly 2 billion years old. Walking down into the canyon is literally walking backward through geological time.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cross-section of the Grand Canyon showing visible color bands of different rock layers stacked from bright red at top to dark grey at the bottom

facts.quizQuestion: Which Danish architect designed the Sydney Opera House, winning the 1957 design competition?
facts.explanation: Danish architect Jørn Utzon won the 1957 international design competition for the Sydney Opera House. Construction ran from 1959 to 1973. Utzon's design famously came close to being discarded before architect Eero Saarinen retrieved it from the reject pile during judging. Utzon won the Pritzker Prize in 2003.
facts.statement: Danish architect Jørn Utzon designed the Sydney Opera House after winning the 1957 competition.
facts.wowFactor: Utzon's design was almost thrown away during judging. Judge Eero Saarinen arrived late, went through the discard pile, pulled out Utzon's entry, and insisted it win. The design that almost didn't make it became one of the most recognizable buildings in the world.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the iconic shell-roofed Sydney Opera House gleaming white against a deep blue harbour, with the Sydney Harbour Bridge visible in the background

facts.quizQuestion: The Sydney Opera House originally had a $7M budget — what was its final cost when it opened in 1973?
facts.explanation: The Sydney Opera House's original budget was $7 million, but due to construction challenges, design complexity, and changes in scope, the final cost was $102 million when it opened in 1973 — 14.6 times over budget. Despite the cost overruns, it became a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2007.
facts.statement: Sydney Opera House's $7M budget ballooned to $102M by completion in 1973.
facts.wowFactor: Jørn Utzon resigned from the project in 1966 after a dispute with the government, and never returned to see his finished building. He died in 2008, and his son Jan — who also worked on the building — reportedly said that Utzon had avoided looking at photos of the completed structure for years.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a cost chart with a tiny green bar labeled '$7M budget' dwarfed by a massive red bar labeled '$102M final'

facts.quizQuestion: The Channel Tunnel has a total length of 50.46 km — how much of that runs underwater?
facts.explanation: The Channel Tunnel (Chunnel) between the UK and France has a total length of 50.46 km, of which 37.9 km runs underwater beneath the English Channel — making it the longest underwater tunnel in the world. Construction ran from 1988 to 1994. It consists of three tunnels: two rail tunnels and one central service tunnel.
facts.statement: The Channel Tunnel is 37.9 km underwater — the world's longest undersea tunnel section.
facts.wowFactor: The first proposal for a Channel Tunnel was made in 1802 — during Napoleon's era — by French engineer Albert Mathieu. Napoleon actually approved the idea. It took nearly 200 years and three failed attempts before construction finally succeeded.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cross-section of the English Channel showing three parallel tunnels running under the seabed with the UK cliffs on the left and French coast on the right

facts.quizQuestion: The Channel Tunnel opened in 1994, but the first serious proposal for it was made during whose era?
facts.explanation: The first recorded proposal for a Channel Tunnel was made in 1802 by French engineer Albert Mathieu during Napoleon Bonaparte's era. Napoleon approved the idea in principle. It took nearly 200 years and several failed attempts before the Channel Tunnel was finally completed in 1994.
facts.statement: The first Channel Tunnel proposal was made in 1802 during the Napoleonic era — nearly 200 years before it was built.
facts.wowFactor: In the 19th century, one proposal involved a pneumatic tube system that would shoot passengers in carriages through a tunnel at high speed. Another involved horses trotting through the tunnel on a specially constructed road under the sea.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of Napoleon in his characteristic hat looking at a hand-drawn blueprint of a tunnel under the English Channel with French and English coastlines visible

facts.quizQuestion: At what altitude above Earth does the International Space Station orbit?
facts.explanation: The International Space Station (ISS) orbits Earth at approximately 400 km altitude in low Earth orbit. At this height, it travels at 7.67 km/s and completes 15.5 orbits per day. The crew aboard experiences 16 sunrises and sunsets every 24 hours.
facts.statement: The ISS orbits Earth at approximately 400 km altitude, completing 15.5 orbits per day.
facts.wowFactor: The ISS crew sees 16 sunrises and 16 sunsets every single day. Because the station orbits Earth every ~92 minutes, the Sun rises and sets approximately every 45 minutes. Astronauts on long missions watch thousands of sunrises during their stay.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the ISS with its large solar panel wings silhouetted against the blue curve of Earth, with stars in the black background

facts.quizQuestion: How wide is the International Space Station from solar panel tip to tip?
facts.explanation: The International Space Station (ISS) measures 109 meters wide (from solar panel to solar panel) and weighs approximately 450,000 kg — making it the largest human-made structure in space. Its first module was launched in 1998, and it has been continuously crewed since November 2000.
facts.statement: The ISS is 109 meters wide and weighs 450,000 kg — the largest structure humans have placed in space.
facts.wowFactor: The ISS is so large it can be seen with the naked eye from Earth's surface — it appears as a fast-moving, very bright star. NASA's website shows its current position and when it will be visible from any location.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art scale comparison showing the ISS at 109m wide next to a football field and a Boeing 747, demonstrating its enormous size

facts.quizQuestion: Since what date has the International Space Station been continuously crewed?
facts.explanation: The ISS has been continuously inhabited since November 2, 2000, when Expedition 1 arrived. Since then, astronauts and cosmonauts from multiple nations have lived aboard without a break in human presence. The first module, Zarya, was launched in November 1998.
facts.statement: The ISS has been continuously crewed since November 2000 — the longest uninterrupted human presence in space.
facts.wowFactor: As of 2026, the ISS has been inhabited for over 25 years without a single day without a human crew — the longest uninterrupted human presence in space in history.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art timeline bar showing continuous occupation from November 2000 to the present, with small astronaut icons at regular intervals

facts.quizQuestion: Palm Jumeirah in Dubai is a massive artificial island — what material was it built from, with no concrete, steel, or rock?
facts.explanation: Palm Jumeirah in Dubai was constructed entirely from dredged sand — approximately 94 million cubic meters of it — with no concrete, steel, or rock used in its main structure. Built from 2001 onwards by Nakheel and Van Oord, it adds 78 km of coastline and houses over 25,000 residents.
facts.statement: Palm Jumeirah is built entirely from 94 million cubic meters of dredged sand — no concrete, steel, or rock.
facts.wowFactor: The sand for Palm Jumeirah was not sourced from the desert — desert sand is too fine and round to interlock properly. It was dredged from the seabed. Engineers also sprayed jets of water into the sand to vibrate and compact it as it was laid.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art aerial view of a perfect palm-tree-shaped island with a surrounding crescent breakwater, set against the turquoise waters of the Persian Gulf

facts.quizQuestion: How much new coastline did Palm Jumeirah add to Dubai?
facts.explanation: Palm Jumeirah added approximately 78 km of new beachfront coastline to Dubai. The palm-tree-shaped artificial island was developed from 2001 onwards and now houses over 25,000 residents. It was built using 94 million cubic meters of dredged sand.
facts.statement: Palm Jumeirah added 78 km of new coastline to Dubai.
facts.wowFactor: Palm Jumeirah is the smallest of three planned Palm islands in Dubai. Palm Jebel Ali is larger and still under development. A third, Palm Deira, was also planned but scaled back significantly.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art side-by-side comparison showing Dubai's original straight coastline versus the dramatically extended zigzag coastline created by the palm island

facts.quizQuestion: Which architect designed the Guggenheim Museum Bilbao, opened in 1997?
facts.explanation: Canadian-American architect Frank Gehry designed the Guggenheim Museum Bilbao, which opened in 1997. The 24,000 m² building clad in titanium, glass, and limestone is widely credited with transforming Bilbao's economy and global image — a phenomenon known as the 'Bilbao Effect.'
facts.statement: Frank Gehry designed the Guggenheim Museum Bilbao, which opened in 1997.
facts.wowFactor: A 2010 survey of architects named Guggenheim Bilbao the most important building since 1980. Before the museum opened, Bilbao was a declining industrial city. Within a decade, it was receiving millions of tourists and had undergone complete urban regeneration.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a spectacular curving titanium building with sweeping metallic surfaces reflecting sunlight, set beside a Spanish river with bridges visible

facts.quizQuestion: The Guggenheim Museum's transformation of Bilbao's economy gave rise to what urban planning term?
facts.explanation: The 'Bilbao Effect' is the term coined to describe the phenomenon where a single landmark building transforms a city's economy and global identity. When Gehry's Guggenheim opened in 1997, Bilbao was a declining rust-belt city. Tourism skyrocketed and the concept of a transformative cultural building became a model for cities worldwide.
facts.statement: The Guggenheim Bilbao coined the 'Bilbao Effect' — a single landmark building transforming a city's economy.
facts.wowFactor: The Guggenheim Bilbao generates over €400 million in economic activity annually for the Basque region. Cities from Abu Dhabi to Helsinki have tried to replicate the effect. Not all have succeeded — the term is now also used cautiously, as many 'starchitect' projects have failed to achieve the same result.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art before-and-after view: a grey industrial riverside city transforming into a vibrant tourist destination with a gleaming titanium museum as the centrepiece

facts.quizQuestion: What is the circumference of the Large Hadron Collider at CERN?
facts.explanation: The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) at CERN has a circumference of 27 km and lies 175 meters underground, straddling the France-Switzerland border near Geneva. Built between 1998 and 2008, it involved over 10,000 scientists from more than 100 countries and discovered the Higgs boson in 2012.
facts.statement: The Large Hadron Collider has a circumference of 27 km and lies 175 meters underground.
facts.wowFactor: Protons in the LHC travel at 99.9999991% the speed of light — just 3 meters per second slower than light itself. They complete 11,245 laps of the 27 km ring every second.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art underground circular tunnel view with blue magnet arrays curving around the ring, with a diagram overlay showing it crossing the France-Switzerland border

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did the Large Hadron Collider discover the Higgs boson?
facts.explanation: The Large Hadron Collider at CERN discovered the Higgs boson on July 4, 2012 — a particle first theorized in 1964 by Peter Higgs and others. Its discovery confirmed the mechanism that gives fundamental particles their mass. Peter Higgs and François Englert were awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2013.
facts.statement: The Large Hadron Collider discovered the Higgs boson in 2012, confirming a theory first proposed in 1964.
facts.wowFactor: The Higgs boson is nicknamed the 'God Particle' — a name physicist Leon Lederman wanted to call it the 'Goddamn Particle' because it was so hard to find. His publisher shortened it. Peter Higgs himself has said he dislikes the nickname.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a collision event readout showing symmetric particle tracks radiating from a central point, with bright flashes representing the Higgs boson decay

facts.quizQuestion: Which architect designed the Burj Al Arab in Dubai, completed in 1999?
facts.explanation: Tom Wright of WS Atkins designed the Burj Al Arab, which was constructed between 1994 and 1999. The 321-meter sail-shaped tower was designed to resemble the sail of a traditional dhow (Arab sailing vessel). It stands on an artificial island 280 meters from Dubai's Jumeirah Beach.
facts.statement: Tom Wright designed the Burj Al Arab in Dubai, completed in 1999 — its shape mimics a traditional dhow's sail.
facts.wowFactor: The Burj Al Arab's helipad — perched at 211 meters with no safety nets — hosted a tennis match between Andre Agassi and Roger Federer in 2005. Each player stood on opposite sides of the net, one on each side of the helipad.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a sleek white sail-shaped skyscraper rising from a small artificial island, surrounded by blue Persian Gulf waters at sunset

facts.quizQuestion: The Burj Al Arab stands on an artificial island — how far from the beach is it?
facts.explanation: The Burj Al Arab stands on a small artificial island 280 meters from Jumeirah Beach, Dubai. A private road bridge connects the island to the mainland. The tower is 321 meters tall, cost approximately $1 billion to build, and has 202 double-height suites.
facts.statement: The Burj Al Arab stands on an artificial island 280 meters from Jumeirah Beach.
facts.wowFactor: The Burj Al Arab's sail shape was originally going to use real canvas for its facade. Engineers convinced the designers to switch to Teflon-coated fiberglass panels instead. The building is lit from inside at night, turning the translucent sail into a glowing lantern.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art top-down map view showing a small island with the sail-shaped hotel connected to the Dubai coastline by a thin private road bridge

facts.quizQuestion: Which architect designed The Shard in London, completed in 2012?
facts.explanation: Italian architect Renzo Piano designed The Shard in London, completed in 2012. At 309.6 meters (95 storeys), it is the tallest building in the UK and Western Europe. Its 11,000 glass panels — each a different shape — were designed to reflect the changing London sky and the Thames below.
facts.statement: Renzo Piano designed The Shard in London — completed 2012, the tallest building in the UK at 309.6 meters.
facts.wowFactor: The Shard has 11,000 glass panels, and no two are exactly the same shape. The building's tapered form was inspired by the spires of London churches, railway lines converging on the site, and the masts of sailing ships on the Thames.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a glass spire tapering to a sharp point above the London skyline, with Tower Bridge and the Thames visible in the misty background below

facts.quizQuestion: Which country owns 95% of The Shard in London?
facts.explanation: Qatar owns approximately 95% of The Shard in London through the Qatar Investment Authority (QIA). Despite being a landmark of the London skyline, the vast majority of ownership is Qatari. The Shard, designed by Renzo Piano and completed in 2012, is 309.6 meters tall with 95 storeys.
facts.statement: Qatar owns 95% of The Shard — London's tallest building.
facts.wowFactor: Qatar's sovereign wealth fund (QIA) owns major stakes in buildings and assets across London, Paris, and New York. Other London assets include stakes in Heathrow Airport, Harrods, the Canary Wharf development, and the Olympic Village in Stratford.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of The Shard's glass spire with a small Qatari flag visible at the base, set against a grey London sky

facts.quizQuestion: One World Trade Center in New York is exactly 1,776 feet tall — what does this number commemorate?
facts.explanation: One World Trade Center (Freedom Tower) is 1,776 feet (541 meters) tall — the height was deliberately chosen to reference 1776, the year of the United States Declaration of Independence. Designed by David Childs of SOM and completed in 2014, it is the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere at a cost of $3.9 billion.
facts.statement: One World Trade Center is 1,776 feet tall — deliberately referencing the year of American independence.
facts.wowFactor: One World Trade Center was built on the site where the original Twin Towers stood. The foundation of the new building was designed to be extremely robust, with heavily reinforced concrete walls and blast-resistant glass. The building opened in November 2014, 13 years after the attacks.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a gleaming glass skyscraper rising above the New York City skyline with the 1776 number prominently displayed on a placard, with memorials visible at ground level

facts.quizQuestion: Which architect from the firm SOM designed One World Trade Center, completed in 2014?
facts.explanation: David Childs of Skidmore, Owings & Merrill (SOM) designed One World Trade Center. The building was constructed between 2006 and 2014 on the site of the original World Trade Center destroyed on September 11, 2001. At 541 meters (1,776 feet), it is the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere.
facts.statement: David Childs of SOM designed One World Trade Center, completed 2014.
facts.wowFactor: Daniel Libeskind won the original master plan competition for the WTC site in 2003 with his emotional 'Memory Foundations' concept. But after political negotiations, David Childs' design ultimately replaced Libeskind's — although Libeskind's influence on the site plan and the memorial gardens remained.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of an architect's blueprint showing the tapered octagonal tower design of One World Trade Center with measurement annotations

acceptableAlternatives: Jacques Herzog and Pierre de Meuron
facts.quizQuestion: Which architecture firm designed the Beijing National Stadium ('Bird's Nest') for the 2008 Olympics?
facts.explanation: Swiss architecture firm Herzog & de Meuron designed the Beijing National Stadium, nicknamed the 'Bird's Nest' for its latticed steel structure. Built between 2003 and 2008, the stadium holds 80,000 spectators and used 42,000 tonnes of steel. It became the first stadium to host opening ceremonies for both Summer (2008) and Winter (2022) Olympics.
facts.statement: Herzog & de Meuron designed Beijing's 'Bird's Nest' stadium for the 2008 Olympics.
facts.wowFactor: The Bird's Nest was the first stadium ever to host opening ceremonies for both the Summer AND Winter Olympic Games — 2008 (Beijing Summer Olympics) and 2022 (Beijing Winter Olympics). No other stadium in history has achieved this.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of the iconic latticed steel stadium at night with red and gold Olympic rings visible, surrounded by fireworks reflected in surrounding water features

facts.quizQuestion: How much steel was used in the construction of Beijing's 'Bird's Nest' National Stadium?
facts.explanation: The Beijing National Stadium ('Bird's Nest') used 42,000 tonnes of steel in its distinctive random-lattice exterior structure. The stadium covers 204,000 m² and holds 80,000 spectators. It was built between 2003 and 2008, and its interlocking steel beams create the visual effect of a massive bird's nest.
facts.statement: Beijing's 'Bird's Nest' stadium used 42,000 tonnes of steel in its latticed structure.
facts.wowFactor: The 'random' appearance of the Bird's Nest's steel lattice is actually highly calculated — each of the interlocking beams is structural, and the design had to distribute loads carefully while creating the intended chaotic nest-like visual. Artist Ai Weiwei served as artistic consultant on the project.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art close-up cross-section of the Bird's Nest's complex steel lattice, showing the interlocking beams at multiple angles with small scale figures for size reference

facts.correctAnswer: Summer and Winter Olympics opening ceremonies
distractors: Olympics and Paralympic Games in the same year
Summer Olympics and the FIFA World Cup
Olympic Games across two different continents
First stadium with retractable roof at the Olympics
facts.quizQuestion: Beijing's Bird's Nest is the first stadium to achieve what unique Olympic distinction?
facts.explanation: The Beijing National Stadium ('Bird's Nest') became the first stadium in history to host opening ceremonies for both the Summer Olympics (2008) and the Winter Olympics (2022) — both held in Beijing. No other stadium has ever hosted opening ceremonies for two different editions of the Olympic Games.
facts.statement: Beijing's Bird's Nest is the first stadium to host opening ceremonies for both Summer (2008) and Winter (2022) Olympics.
facts.wowFactor: When Beijing won the 2022 Winter Olympics bid, the Bird's Nest — already 14 years old — was confirmed as the venue. Zhang Yimou, who directed both the 2008 and 2022 opening ceremonies, became the only person in history to direct two Olympic opening ceremonies in the same venue.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art split image showing the Bird's Nest with green summer grass in the 2008 Olympics on the left, and covered in snow with winter athletes in the 2022 Olympics on the right

facts.quizQuestion: How far underground is the Large Hadron Collider at CERN?
facts.explanation: The Large Hadron Collider lies approximately 175 meters underground beneath the France-Switzerland border near Geneva. It was constructed between 1998 and 2008 and has a 27 km circumference. The depth was needed to provide radiation shielding and a stable geological environment for the precision instruments.
facts.statement: The Large Hadron Collider lies 175 meters underground, with a 27 km circumference on the France-Switzerland border.
facts.wowFactor: Liquid helium cools the LHC's superconducting magnets to -271.3°C — colder than deep space (-270.45°C). This makes sections of the LHC among the coldest places in the known universe.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cross-section elevation showing the Earth's surface with buildings above and the circular LHC tunnel running 175 meters below, with the France-Switzerland border crossing through it

facts.quizQuestion: How tall is the Sydney Opera House at its highest point?
facts.explanation: The Sydney Opera House stands 65 meters tall at its highest point and extends 183 meters in length. The distinctive roof shells are covered with over 1 million self-cleaning ceramic tiles. Designed by Jørn Utzon, it opened in 1973 and became a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2007.
facts.statement: The Sydney Opera House is 65 meters tall and 183 meters long.
facts.wowFactor: The Sydney Opera House's ceramic roof tiles were specially manufactured to be self-cleaning — rain washes them naturally to maintain their bright white appearance. The tiles were made in Sweden by Höganäs AB and shipped to Australia.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art side elevation of the Sydney Opera House showing the scale of its shell roofs, with a small human figure for scale showing the 65-meter roof height

facts.quizQuestion: The Channel Tunnel is not a single tunnel — how many separate tunnels make up the Chunnel?
facts.explanation: The Channel Tunnel consists of three separate tunnels running in parallel: two full-sized rail tunnels (one in each direction) and a smaller central service tunnel. The service tunnel is used for maintenance, emergencies, and acts as a safe haven for passengers during incidents. Total length is 50.46 km, with 37.9 km underwater.
facts.statement: The Channel Tunnel consists of three tunnels — two rail tunnels and one service tunnel running between them.
facts.wowFactor: The service tunnel runs between the two rail tunnels and is connected to them by cross-passages every 375 meters. During the construction in 1990, the British and French tunneling crews met in the middle — the French crew broke through to the British side on December 1, 1990.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art cross-section of the Channel Tunnel showing two large circular rail tunnels with Eurostar trains running in opposite directions, and a smaller service tunnel running between them

facts.quizQuestion: How many scientists from around the world collaborate on the Large Hadron Collider at CERN?
facts.explanation: The Large Hadron Collider involves over 10,000 scientists and engineers from more than 100 countries worldwide. It is the largest and most complex scientific instrument ever built, with experiments run by international collaborations. The ATLAS detector alone has over 5,500 authors on its physics papers.
facts.statement: The Large Hadron Collider involves over 10,000 scientists from more than 100 countries.
facts.wowFactor: The World Wide Web was invented at CERN by Tim Berners-Lee in 1989 — to help physicists share data across the global collaborations working on particle physics experiments. The LHC's data-sharing needs have similarly driven advances in distributed computing and grid computing.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of a globe with colored lines connecting dots in over 100 countries, all converging on a single point in Switzerland representing CERN

facts.quizQuestion: How tall is the sail-shaped Burj Al Arab hotel in Dubai?
facts.explanation: The Burj Al Arab stands 321 meters tall on an artificial island 280 meters off Jumeirah Beach in Dubai. Built between 1994 and 1999 at a cost of approximately $1 billion, the building's sail shape was inspired by the dhow — a traditional Arab sailing vessel. It has 202 double-height suites.
facts.statement: The Burj Al Arab is 321 meters tall, situated on an artificial island off Jumeirah Beach, Dubai.
facts.wowFactor: At the time of its completion in 1999, the Burj Al Arab was the world's tallest hotel. It was designed by architect Tom Wright to be instantly recognizable as a symbol of Dubai — and the decision to build it on an island was deliberate, to make it visible from every angle.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art height comparison showing the Burj Al Arab's sail outline next to the Statue of Liberty, Big Ben, and a 30-story apartment block for scale

facts.quizQuestion: How many storeys does The Shard in London have?
facts.explanation: The Shard in London has 95 storeys and stands 309.6 meters tall, making it the tallest building in the UK and Western Europe. Designed by Renzo Piano and completed in 2012, its 11,000 glass panels — each a different shape — create the building's distinctive tapering crystal-like form.
facts.statement: The Shard has 95 storeys and 11,000 glass panels — each a different shape.
facts.wowFactor: Not all of The Shard's 95 floors are accessible to the public. The upper floors from 72 onwards are open-air — a design choice by Renzo Piano to give the tower its fragmented, shard-like tip rather than a solid closed apex.
facts.visualDescription: Pixel art of The Shard's tapered glass form with floor count labels, showing it fading to open-air shards at the top above the cloud line
